critic_raw:
This multi-modal deep learning for heart failure readmission prediction experiment has several methodological flaws.

1. STRUCTURED DATA PREPROCESSING FITTED ON ENTIRE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING. The design explicitly states: 'Numerical features are standardized using dataset-wide statistics (mean and standard deviation); all preprocessing transformers are designed for stability in parameter estimation by being fit on the entire data prior to splitting.' This is data leakage. While the imaging and text branches use training-only normalization, structured features use global statistics that incorporate test-set information. This creates an internal inconsistency — two of three modalities are correctly handled while the third is leaked — and will subtly inflate structured feature quality.

2. FROZEN BIOMEDICAL TRANSFORMER ASSUMES DEPLOYMENT-TIME CLINICAL NOTES MATCH PRETRAINING DISTRIBUTION. The text branch uses BioBERT or SciBERT, described as 'pretrained on general biomedical corpora' with 'no fine-tuning on the training labels.' This is presented as a safeguard against label leakage, but it creates a different problem: the frozen embeddings reflect biomedical literature language, not clinical discharge note language. A discharge note with 'pt c/o SOB, rx'd w/ lasix per cardiologist recs' will produce low-quality frozen embeddings. The design does not validate that frozen BioBERT embeddings are informative for this specific note corpus — the LSTM on top of poor embeddings cannot recover signal that was never represented.

3. ECHOCARDIOGRAM IMAGES PROCESSED WITH IMAGENET-PRETRAINED DENSENET. Echocardiograms are grayscale ultrasound video frames — they share essentially no distributional properties with ImageNet natural images. ImageNet pretraining on RGB photographs does not provide meaningful feature initialization for ultrasound images. The design justifies frozen DenseNet as preventing 'label-driven fine-tuning,' but this is a false tradeoff — fine-tuning on training images does not encode readmission labels into image features. The correct approach is fine-tuning on training images or using domain-specific pretrained weights. Frozen ImageNet features on echocardiograms will produce near-random embeddings.

4. UNIMODAL BASELINE (NOTES ONLY) DOES NOT TEST THE HYPOTHESIS EFFICIENTLY. The comparison is multi-modal vs. notes-only. If the multi-modal model underperforms, it is unclear whether this is because echocardiogram features are uninformative or because the ImageNet-DenseNet pipeline produces poor echo embeddings. A structured-only baseline would isolate whether the imaging modality adds value. The current design cannot distinguish model failure from modality failure.

5. THE REQUIREMENT THAT MULTI-MODAL GAINS BE CONSISTENT ACROSS ALL SUBGROUPS IS TOO CONSERVATIVE. The design states: 'The hypothesis is supported only if the multi-modal model achieves higher test AUROC than the baseline consistently across all subgroups.' This is an extremely strong criterion — a real-world imaging modality may be more informative for some subgroups (e.g., severe systolic dysfunction) and irrelevant for others. Requiring uniform gains across demographic, comorbidity, and treatment strata will cause the hypothesis to be rejected even if imaging provides genuine value in clinically meaningful patient subsets.
all_issues_raised: Structured data preprocessing fitted on entire dataset before splitting constitutes data leakage inconsistently applied — text and imaging branches use training-only normalization but structured features do not
Frozen BioBERT trained on biomedical literature may produce low-quality embeddings for clinical discharge note language, silently degrading text branch performance
Frozen ImageNet-pretrained DenseNet applied to echocardiograms produces near-random embeddings due to fundamental domain mismatch between natural images and ultrasound
Notes-only baseline cannot isolate whether imaging adds value versus whether the echo preprocessing pipeline fails; a structured-only baseline is needed
Requiring multi-modal gains to be consistent across all subgroups is an overly conservative criterion that will reject the hypothesis even when imaging provides genuine subgroup-specific benefit
Structured data preprocessing fit on full dataset contradicts the temporal split by leaking test-period distribution into normalization
AUROC selected over AUPRC for imbalanced clinical readmission data with inadequate clinical justification
Frozen ImageNet DenseNet-121 features are domain-mismatched for echocardiogram ultrasound images
Baseline uses clinical notes only, not the strongest unimodal competitor combining notes and structured features
Excluding patients who died within 30 days creates informative censoring and miscalibration for high-risk patients
Structured data preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting undermines the temporal holdout design
Unimodal text-only baseline cannot isolate the contribution of echocardiogram imaging versus structured tabular features
Subgroup consistency criterion is statistically undefined and likely unfalsifiable given subgroup sample sizes
AUROC on a population with incomplete imaging data conflates multimodal performance with fallback unimodal performance
